2: havel havalim amar qohelet havel havalim hakol havel: 'Vanity of vanities, says the Preacher, vanity of vanities! All is vanity.' The Hebrew hevel (vapor, breath, vanity) is used 38 times in Ecclesiastes — more than in any other biblical book. It literally means a breath of air that passes immediately: something that exists momentarily and then is gone. The LXX translates hevel as mataiotes (futility, vanity), and Paul uses this word in Romans 8:20: 'the creation was subjected to futility [mataiotes].' The Ecclesiastes diagnosis is therefore not pessimism but realism about the post-fall condition of creation: all earthly striving that does not account for God and eternity is, sub specie aeternitatis, vapor. The NT's response is the resurrection, which gives permanence to what was formerly vapor: 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Cor 15:58).
1: Dibrei Qohelet ben-David melek biYerushalayim — 'The words of Qohelet, son of David, king in Jerusalem.' The title introduces the speaker by function rather than name: Qohelet (H6953) is a feminine participial noun from qahal (to assemble), meaning 'the one who assembles' or 'the preacher/assembler of an audience.' The feminine form is unusual for a title referring to a male king — it may function as an abstract office title (cf. Sophereth, Ezra 2:55). The Solomonic frame ('son of David, king in Jerusalem') is never made fully explicit by name; 'Qohelet' as speaker is maintained throughout. This allows the book to function as royal wisdom literature without being locked to Solomon's historical particulars.
3: Mah-yitron la'adam — 'What lasting gain does a person have?' The word yitron (H3504) is used ten times in Ecclesiastes and almost nowhere else in the OT (once in Exod 16:23 in an unrelated sense). It carries a commercial nuance: 'surplus,' 'profit,' 'net advantage after deducting costs.' The Teacher is asking whether there is a net positive return on all human investment. The prepositional phrase betahat hashemesh ('under the sun') appears 29 times in Ecclesiastes and is the book's self-imposed limit: it marks the sub-eschatological perspective, what can be known within the frame of mortal, earthly existence without resurrection or final judgment. The question of yitron is therefore precisely bounded: not 'is there value in life?' but 'is there net advantage within the closed system of existence under the sun?'
4: Dor holekh ve-dor ba ve-ha'aretz le'olam amedet — 'One generation goes and another generation comes, but the earth remains forever.' The contrast is structurally precise: human generations are characterized by movement — holekh (going) and ba (coming) — while the earth amedet (stands firm, endures). The verb amad for the earth's permanence contrasts with the transience of dor (generation). The phrase le'olam ('forever') here means 'continuously, without end' — it characterizes the earth's persistence, not its ultimate ontological status. The irony is that what appears most solid and permanent (the earth) outlasts what considers itself most significant (successive human generations).
5: Ve-zarach hashemesh uba hashemesh — 'The sun rises and the sun sets.' The root zarach (to rise/shine) is used for both sunrise and the dawn of glory elsewhere (Isa 60:1-2: 'arise, shine, for your light has come'). Here it merely marks the relentless repetition of the solar circuit. El-miqomo shoef zarach sham — 'hurrying to its place where it rises' — the verb sha'af means to pant or gasp (used for the panting of animals). The sun is personified as a gasping runner who cannot stop. The Teacher's rhetoric deliberately flattens what would normally be celebrated (sunrise) into evidence of endless futile repetition.
6: Holekh el-darom ve-sovev el-tzafon sovev sovev holekh haruach — 'The wind blows south, then turns north; it whirls around and around.' The word ruach (H7307) means both 'wind' and 'spirit/breath.' This double meaning runs through Ecclesiastes: the wind's aimless circulation (1:6) and 'chasing the wind' (1:14, re'ut ruach) anticipate the deep question of ch 3:21: 'Who knows whether the spirit [ruach] of man goes upward?' The same word that describes futile circular motion (wind circling) describes what is potentially immortal in the human person. Ve-al-sevivotav shav haruach — 'the wind returns to its circuits' — sevivotav from savav (to go around/turn), the same root as the wind's 'whirling'; there is no progress, only return.
7: Kol-hanechalim holkhim el-hayam ve-hayam einenu male — 'All the rivers run to the sea, yet the sea is never full.' The image is of an apparently purposeful process (rivers flowing toward an obvious destination) that produces no net result (the sea fills no further). El-maqom shehanechalim holkhim sham hem shavim laleshet — 'to the place where the rivers go, they return to flow again.' The verb shav (to return) describes both a physical cycle and a moral one throughout the wisdom literature: the righteous 'return' to God (Ps 90:3), the fool 'returns' to his folly (Prov 26:11). Here the rivers' return is merely hydrological — they evaporate, condense, and fall again — but the Teacher uses this mechanistic cycle as an image for the futility of all output that produces no permanent gain.
8: Kol-hadvarim ye'eim — 'All things are wearisome' or 'All words are wearisome.' The noun devarim (H1697) means both 'words/speech' and 'things/matters' — the ambiguity may be deliberate: both the things of life and the words used to describe them exhaust without satisfying. Lo yuchal ish ledaber — 'no one can fully express it' — the verb dibber connects back to devarim: the things are too wearisome to speak of, yet also the words are what prove insufficient. Lo-tiśba ayin lir'ot ve-lo-timale ozen mishmo'a — 'the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear full with hearing.' Sava (satisfied/full) links to the sea that is never 'full' (v.7, einenu male): the same Hebrew root family describes the fullness the sea never achieves and the satisfaction the eye never reaches.
9: Mah-shehayah hu sheyih-yeh — 'What has been is what will be.' The construction is emphatic repetition: shehayah (what has been) = sheyih-yeh (what will be). This is absolute cyclical determinism stated as wisdom observation. Ve-ein kol-chadash tahat hashemesh — 'there is nothing new under the sun.' Chadash (new) is the same root as in the covenant promise 'I will make a new covenant' (Jer 31:31) and the eschatological promise 'I make all things new' (Rev 21:5/Isa 43:19). The Teacher's claim is precisely bounded by his 'under the sun' frame — the new thing that breaks the cycle cannot appear within the closed system of the mortal world; the Teacher is right about the earthly frame and wrong only in that he cannot see beyond it.
10: Yesh davar sheyomer re'eh zeh chadash hu — 'Is there a thing of which one says, ‘See, this is new’?' The rhetorical question pattern is characteristic of Ecclesiastes' diatribe style: an imagined objector is entertained and then dismissed. Kebar hayah le'olamim — 'It has already been in the ages before us.' Olamim (plural of olam) = 'the ages' or 'antiquity' — time stretching back before memory. The Teacher's logic: what appears new to the present generation is merely beyond living memory (cf. v.11). This is observational wisdom, not philosophical nihilism; its limits become clear in v.11.
11: Ein zikaron larishonim — 'There is no remembrance of earlier people.' The noun zikaron (H2146) — memorial, remembrance, record — is related to zakhar (to remember), the root of the covenant promise 'I will remember my covenant' (Gen 9:15; Exod 2:24). YHWH's remembrance is what sustains the covenant when human memory fails. The Teacher identifies what is lost 'under the sun': no human mechanism exists to preserve memory across generations. Ve-gam barishonim shehyu — 'nor will there be among those yet to come' — the symmetry is complete: past generations are forgotten and future generations will forget us. This is the anthropological situation that the covenant's memorial practices (zikaron, the Passover, the Lord's Supper) exist to address.
12: Ani Qohelet hayiti melek al-Yisrael biYerushalayim — 'I, the Teacher, was king over Israel in Jerusalem.' The past tense hayiti ('I was') has puzzled interpreters: if the Teacher is currently king, why past tense? Some take it as a literary device (Qohelet is not actually Solomon, but a wisdom persona using his voice after-the-fact). Others read it as Solomon writing from old age looking back. The uncertainty is part of the book's rhetorical design: the Solomonic frame gives the experiment maximum epistemic authority (who could have done this experiment better?) without claiming to be Solomon's straightforward autobiography. The past tense emphasizes completed experience: 'I have been king — I know from the inside.'
13: Ve-natati et-libbi lidrosh ve-latour bahokmah — 'I applied my heart to seek and to search out by wisdom.' The verb pair darash (seek/inquire) + tur (explore/scout) describes intensive, systematic investigation — the same verbs used for the spies who scouted Canaan (Num 13:25). Inyan ra' hu asher natan Elohim livnei ha'adam la'anot bo — 'It is an unhappy business that God has given to the children of man to be busy with.' Inyan (H6045) is nearly unique to Ecclesiastes (occurs 8 times here, once in Sirach, nowhere else in the OT canon). It means business/occupation/task. The characterization of this investigating task as ra' (evil/unhappy) is the Teacher's verdict on what it is to be a questing human under the sun: God has given us a vocation of inquiry that, within the earthly frame, leads only to the recognition of limits.
14: Ra'iti et-kol-hama'asim sheassho tahat hashemesh — 'I have seen all the works that are done under the sun.' The verb ra'ah (to see/observe) is the Teacher's characteristic cognitive tool — used ~47 times in Ecclesiastes. He is an empiricist: he tests claims by observation. Re'ut ruach — 'chasing the wind' (or 'feeding on wind' — the verb ra'ah can mean 'to pasture/feed'). This phrase appears throughout chs. 1-6 as the verdict on each futile enterprise. The double meaning — chasing what cannot be caught, feeding on what has no substance — captures the Teacher's tone. The wind cannot be grabbed or accumulated; the labor that produces no lasting gain is as insubstantial as attempting to hold wind in one's fist.
15: Me'uvat lo-yuchal litkon — 'What is crooked cannot be made straight.' The verb taqan (to make straight/set right) appears only 3 times in the OT, two of them in Ecclesiastes (1:15; 7:13). The passive participle me'uvat (twisted/bent) describes a condition imposed from outside — the world as it currently is has been bent. The Teacher does not explain why the world is bent (that would require Gen 3's frame), but he recognizes the datum: within the 'under the sun' perspective, the crooked cannot be straightened by human wisdom. Ve-hemron lo yuchal lehimanol — 'and what is lacking cannot be counted' — hemron is a hapax legomenon (occurs once only); the sense is 'what is missing/deficient.' The Teacher identifies a fundamental insufficiency in the created order as he experiences it.
16: Hibarthi ani im-libbi lemor ani hineh higdalti ve-horafti hokmah al-kol asher-hayah lefanai al-Yerushalayim — 'I said to myself, “I have acquired more wisdom than anyone who ruled before me.”' The self-address (dibarthi ani im-libbi = 'I spoke with my heart') is one of Ecclesiastes' characteristic rhetorical devices; the 'heart' is the seat of the self, and the Teacher's internal dialogue becomes the structure of his investigation. The boast 'more than anyone before me in Jerusalem' maximizes the scope of the experiment: if Solomonic wisdom cannot find yitron, no wisdom can. Ve-libbi ra'ah harbeh hokmah ve-da'at — 'my heart has had great experience of wisdom and knowledge' — da'at (knowledge) is paired with hokmah (wisdom) as the two cognitive virtues; the Teacher has pursued both to the maximum.
17: Ve-etna libbi lada'at hokmah ve-lada'at holalot ve-sikhlet — 'I applied my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly.' The pairing of wisdom with its opposites — holalot (madness/acting-foolishly) and sikhlet (folly/stupidity) — shows the Teacher's comprehensive methodology: he does not study wisdom in isolation but in contrast with its extremes. Yada'ti shegam-zeh hu ra'yon ruach — 'I perceived that this also is chasing the wind.' The word ra'yon is a variant form of re'ut (pursuit/striving) — both appear in Ecclesiastes for 'chasing the wind.' The conclusion: the pursuit of wisdom itself, as an end in itself, within the 'under the sun' frame, produces the same verdict. Wisdom is good but insufficient to achieve yitron.
18: Ki be-rov hokmah rov ka'as — 'For in much wisdom is much vexation.' The word ka'as (H3708) means grief, vexation, anger — the emotional state of frustration. Wisdom, the Teacher finds, does not deliver contentment; it delivers the expanded capacity to perceive what is wrong with the world, which is its own form of suffering. Ve-yosef da'at yosef makhov — 'and whoever increases knowledge increases sorrow.' The verb yosef (to add/increase) also spells the name Joseph — a word-play tradition observed in rabbinic commentary, though the primary meaning is straightforward. Makhov (H4341) means grief/pain — physical or emotional. The paradox: the cure (wisdom and knowledge) exacerbates the disease (awareness of futility). This is not anti-intellectualism but a precise observation about the limits of wisdom within the post-fall, sub-eschatological frame.
